Inside the Public Library's Digital Preservation Lab

By Renee Alvarez

In a secured room below the main reading hall, archivists process hard drives, CDs, and memory cards from retired city departments. The preservation lab treats each device as a historical object, documenting labels, ownership notes, and chain-of-custody details before a single file is copied.

Engineers then create forensic disk images and run checksum validation to detect corruption early. If a source contains obsolete file formats, the team stores both the original and a migration copy, allowing future researchers to verify any transformation performed during access preparation.

Oral-history interviews make up the fastest-growing collection. Staff describe a hybrid workflow where transcripts are corrected by volunteers, then linked to timecoded audio so search tools can jump directly to meaningful moments in a recording.

Lab director Naomi Fields says the biggest challenge is scale, not technology. New data arrives daily from community organizations, and each donation requires legal review, rights documentation, and a metadata profile that can survive decades of software change.
